data.title: Paysafe: Reported Revenue Returns To Growth In Q3 2025
data.description:
Introduction

Paysafe Limited (PSFE) slumped a stunning 27.56% on November 13, 2025, with weakness in technology stocks and a negative reaction to the company's Q3 2025 earnings creating the perfect storm. When one considers the company's debt-heavy capital structure, however, the move in the company's valuation is not as dramatic.

Case in point, according to Seeking Alpha data, PSFE ended 2024 with an enterprise value of about $3.27 billion, with recent share price weakness and debt reductions taking the enterprise value to about $2.72 billion, or a drop of 16.8% year-to-date (my calculations show a marginally higher enterprise value of $2.85 billion, indicating a slightly smaller enterprise value drop of 12.8%):

For comparison, PSFE's largest peer PayPal Holdings (PYPL) has seen its enterprise value decline by roughly 28% so far in 2025, indicating that on a company-wide basis, Paysafe is still faring better, which is not the conclusion you would get if you look at share prices alone, with Paysafe stock slumping roughly 57% year-to-date, well above the ~23% decline for PayPal:

Paysafe's Q3 2025 results confirm a return to topline growth in USD terms, as elaborated on in my previous coverage from August 2025. While adjusted EBITDA margins and free cash flow conversion rates are near-term negatives, I confirm my Buy rating on Paysafe, driven by:

  • An attractive 9.3% free cash flow yield to enterprise value, increasing to approximately 21.8% when looking at free cash flow to equity.
  • The prospect of a normalization in free cash flow conversion rates to about 65-70%.
  • Fed rate cuts in 2026 increasing the portion of free cash flow reaching common shareholders in 2026-2027.

In this article, I will expand on these points, provide an update how Paysafe's valuation stands relative to PayPal, and discuss risks to the investment case.

Company Overview

You can access all PSFE results on its website here. Paysafe provides a variety of payment services with a focus on the entertainment sector (primarily gaming and gambling). The company reports results in two key segments - Merchant Solutions at 53% of Q3 2025 revenues and Digital Wallets at 47%.

While the two segments are roughly comparable from a revenue standpoint, profitability in Digital Wallets is much higher, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 45%, versus just 21% in Merchant Solutions.

I calculate the company's enterprise value at roughly $2.85 billion, of which 21% is funded with common equity, leaving 79% for net debt.

Q3 2025 Results Overview

Paysafe reported revenue growth in USD terms of 2% Y/Y in Q3 2025. Organic revenue growth, which adjusts for currency effects and interest income, stood at 6% Y/Y. As expected, sales growth translated into better margins, with the adjusted EBITDA margin improving to 29.2%, up 1.6% Y/Y. The only notable disappointment was lower unlevered free cash flow, which slipped 7% Y/Y to $83.6 million in the quarter.

The silver lining with regards to the free cash flow decline is that it was partially driven by higher capex (which is good for future growth), but other than that, cash flow from operations was indeed quite weak at $69.2 million, down 15.5% Y/Y. CFO John Crawford cited timing of receivables and tax refunds in the prior-year quarter as contributing factors.

Setting the weaker free cash flow aside, I believe Q3 2025 results were quite robust. Organic revenue growth, at 6% Y/Y, was stronger than the 5% increase in Q2. Coupled with higher capex, I believe this bodes well for the company's trajectory in Q4 2025 and going into 2026.

2025 Guidance Reduced

While revenue growth did accelerate in Q3 2025, the jump was apparently somewhat below management expectations, with Paysafe reducing its full-year revenue outlook to $1,700-$1,710 million, down 1% relative to the prior outlook. Still, when we factor in that the company reported revenues of $420 million in Q4 2024, the company's guidance envisages growth in USD terms of about 5% Y/Y in Q4 2025, which again I think is quite encouraging:

Weaker revenues will naturally translate into lower adjusted EBITDA, which is now expected at $425-$430 million, down 9% relative to the prior midpoint. This will result in a weaker adjusted EBITDA margin, as discussed on the conference call:

Adjusted EBITDA increased 7% to $126.6 million. Our third quarter results benefited from a licensing deal, which contributed approximately $10 million to revenue and adjusted EBITDA, offsetting the headwind from ongoing business mix and the divestiture, which has less impact in the second half. As a result, adjusted EBITDA margin was 29.2%, up 160 basis points year-over-year. Given what we're continuing to see at the gross margin level for both segments, we don't expect EBITDA margins to be at this level in Q4. We expect adjusted EBITDA margins closer to 23% in Q4 and about 25% for the full year.

Overall, I think it is encouraging to see topline and organic revenue growth converging in Q4 2025, although margin weakness is a clear disappointment with regards to near-term financial performance. Still, with a strong revenue trajectory going into year-end 2025, I believe margins should begin to recover in 2026, as confirmed on the conference call:

Overall, this is not where we plan to be from a margin perspective, nor does it reflect the potential of our business model and the operating leverage that we can deliver.

Paysafe Valuation

As discussed in the Introduction section, employing an enterprise-level valuation approach is, in my opinion, the best course of action, justifying the company's very high leverage.

Over the past 12 months, unlevered free cash flow stands at $265.3 million, reflecting a 62% conversion of Adjusted EBITDA into free cash flow:

I should note the conversion in Q3 2025 stood at 66%, with 65-70% expected for Q4 2025. As such, the $265.3 million is likely a depressed number, but conservatively, I will use it in valuation.

Relative to the $2.85 billion enterprise value, we observe that the free cash flow yield stands at roughly 9.3%, which, for a company growing revenues in line with inflation, is overall attractive in my opinion.

Compared with PayPal, which boasts an adjusted free cash flow of about $6.4 billion over the past 12 months (free cash flow yield to enterprise value of roughly 10%), we observe that Paysafe is now more expensive. What's more, Paysafe's larger peer delivered stronger revenue growth, at 7% Y/Y in Q3 2025.

That said, I still believe that Paysafe's valuation is appealing in itself, especially if we assume a marginally stronger free cash flow conversion in 2026. Hence, I still believe the company is worth a Buy rating. Even so, the investment case is now more blurred as Paysafe is trading marginally above the valuation of PayPal, indicating a normalization in free cash flow conversion is already priced in.

Paysafe is furthermore quite attractive for investors with above-average risk tolerance, as free cash flow to equity stands at roughly $130 million over the past 12 months, or a free cash flow yield of roughly 21.8%. This attractiveness is further confirmed by ongoing share repurchases, with the company increasing its repurchase authorization by $70 million alongside Q3 2025 results.

Risks

79% of Paysafe's enterprise value is funded with net debt, making the company exposed to changes in interest rates as well as creditors' perception of its creditworthiness. The good news for Paysafe is that the Fed funds futures price in a Fed funds rate of about 3% by the end of 2026, indicating interest costs, at 5.1% currently, should decline marginally in 2026 and 2027. This, in turn, will allow a greater proportion of free cash flow to reach common shareholders, increasing the already attractive 21.8% common stock free cash flow yield.

As discussed in the Paysafe Valuation section, some normalization in free cash flow conversion is already priced in. Hence, a persistently low free cash flow conversion relative to adjusted EBITDA, such as the 62% observed over the past 12 months, would lead to further share price declines.

Last but not least, while revenues are still growing at the moment, the sector-wide decline in valuations seems to indicate investors are worried about performance in future years, perhaps driven by the emergence of AI/crypto-based competitors, which will reduce the demand for legacy payment systems.

Conclusion

While Paysafe's year-to-date share price decline is dramatic, I believe it is largely the result of significant leverage and a drop in sector-wide valuations. That said, investors are clearly worried about weakness in free cash flow conversion and weaker margins going into Q4 2025.

Looking ahead to 2026, topline growth in USD will increasingly converge with organic growth, although Fed rate cuts will likely result in incremental weakness in reported revenue growth.

I confirm my Buy rating on Paysafe, with the company offering an attractive 21.8% free cash flow yield to equity, which is set to increase amid Fed rate cuts and business growth.

Key risks in the investment case include high leverage, no normalization in free cash flow conversion, and the emergence of disruptive AI/crypto-based payment competitors.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: FDLO: Leading The Low-Volatility Category, But Lagging The Benchmark
data.description:
This article updates my review published in April 2025 in light of recent performance and current holdings.

FDLO strategy

Fidelity Low Volatility Factor ETF (FDLO) was launched on 09/12/2016 and tracks the Fidelity U.S. Low Volatility Factor Index. FDLO has a portfolio of 129 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.30%, and a total expense ratio of 0.16%. Distributions are paid quarterly.

The mutual fund Fidelity U.S. Low Volatility Equity Fund (FULVX) is planned to be merged into FDLO on or about 5/8/2026. FULVX shareholders would receive corresponding shares of FDLO. It will not result in a large increase in FDLO’s assets under management (already over $1.4 billion), as FULVX has less than $40 million in AUM.

The fund aims to generate returns similar to the broader market in the long term with less volatility. The purpose is to increase risk-adjusted performance by reducing risk. As described by Fidelity, the largest 1,000 U.S. stocks based on market cap are screened for low historical volatility of prices and earnings based on three metrics in equal weight:

  • 5-year Standard Deviation of Price Returns,
  • 5-year Beta,
  • 5-year Standard Deviation of EPS.

Constituents are selected and weighted by an iterative process considering the factor score, sectors, and market capitalization. The index is rebalanced semi-annually. This article will use as a benchmark the Russell 1000 Index, represented by iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB).

Portfolio

FDLO is almost exclusively invested in U.S.-domiciled companies (99% of asset value), mostly in large and mega-caps (78%). The sector breakdown of FDLO is very close to the benchmark’s, as plotted on the next chart, pointing to a sector-neutral weighting methodology. The portfolio is overweight in technology with 34.1% of assets, while other sectors are below 14%.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, with 39% of asset value in the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table. The three heaviest positions (Apple, Microsoft, and Alphabet) weigh between 5% and 8%. Company risk is similar to that of the Russell 1000, where the top four issuers are between 6% and 7%.

data.title: IDE: This Industrials, Infrastructure, And Materials Fund Has Growth Potential, 10% Yield
data.description:
As a now retired income investor, I follow the macro events that dictate which investments may perform better at various times throughout the economic cycle. One fund that I have on my watchlist that is taking advantage of current and future macro trends is the Voya Infrastructure Industrials and Materials Fund (IDE). As the name implies, this CEF (closed-end fund) invests in the equities of companies that are primarily involved in the Industrials, Infrastructure, and Basic Materials market sectors. According to the fund website, the IDE fund:

  • Invests primarily in companies in the infrastructure, industrials and materials sectors that will potentially benefit from the building, renovation, expansion and utilization of infrastructure
  • Seeks to build a diversified portfolio of equity securities of companies that may potentially benefit from spending in six areas: power, construction, materials, communications, transportation and water

In addition, the fund sells call options on various ETFs with the underlying value of such options representing anywhere from 15% to 50% of the total underlying value of the fund portfolio.

In the past five years, the total return performance of IDE has matched that of the S&P 500 (SP500), partly due to the monthly high-yield distribution.

Yet today, the fund trades at a wider than average discount of about -8% while offering a 10% current yield based on a monthly distribution of $0.10 per share. The fund discount to NAV narrowed over the past year up until September and has recently widened back out again to what appears to be a good entry point for those interested in this multi-faceted income fund. The chart below from CEFConnect illustrates the recent price action that has led to a drop in the market price while the NAV has been rising over the past several months, resulting in a widening of the discount.

IDE is a global fund with only about 47% of fund holdings based in the U.S. as of 9/30/25 as shown on the website.

The fund’s inception date was January 26, 2010 and currently has about $194 million in net assets consisting of 240 total holdings. Top holdings include several well-established large cap global companies in a number of diverse sectors.

Sector weightings are also shown on the website and give more diversity to the fund to help level out the risk/reward balance, weighing more heavily toward aerospace and defense companies, electric utilities, and electrical component and machinery suppliers as of 9/30/25.

From the fund’s Q3 '25 market commentary, I found some useful insights into recent and potential future fund performance. First of all, the fund strategy is outlined:

The Fund seeks potential enhanced stability of returns over a market cycle through investments in global companies with potential to benefit from increased infrastructure spending, and by selling call options on select equity Indexes or exchange-traded funds.

During the quarter, the fund delivered a total return of 7.11% on a NAV basis and 6.35% on the basis of market price. For the quarter, the equity sleeve outperformed the index and stock selection in industrials and utilities contributed to outperformance while stock selection within information technology and communications services detracted. The option strategy had a negative impact during the quarter.

Fund performance has been very good over the past 1-year, 3-year and 5-year periods with a 10.3% annual return at market price over the past ten years. The YTD return (as of 9/30/25) of 28.3% highlights the recent momentum that is driving strong performance in 2025 and that appears likely to continue into 2026.

The current strategy and outlook offer useful details to consider.

Emerging markets outperformed. Taiwan and Korea advanced thanks to the semiconductor boom, offsetting weakness in China and slower growth in Latin America and Africa. Investors became more selective later in the quarter, favoring countries with strong finances and clear policies.

Developed markets saw broad, steady growth. European industrials, banks, and energy companies benefited from improving economic data, while defensive sectors in Switzerland and the United Kingdom helped limit volatility. The weaker U.S. dollar boosted returns for U.S. investors, lifted market confidence. Value stocks gained momentum in banks and energy, while quality stocks stayed resilient as inflation eased.

Looking ahead, emerging markets could stay strong if global borrowing costs remain low and demand for technology and raw materials continues. Stable policies in China and healthy consumer spending worldwide will be important to keeping that growth going.

Distribution History and Coverage

As you can see on the five-year dividend history chart, IDE has paid a consistent and growing distribution, quarterly at $0.23 per share until April 2024 when they changed to monthly and increased to $0.10 where it has continued until today.

According to the latest section 19 notice for the October distribution, the majority of the YTD payouts have consisted of NII and capital gains, with only about 23% ROC. The past several months have included zero ROC, which would suggest that the distribution is well covered by NII and primarily from capital gains.

It’s no surprise that the fund is realizing some substantial long-term capital gains when looking at a 5-year total return chart of the fund’s top holdings. General Electric (GE), Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY), and Arista Networks (ANET) in particular have delivered very high total return performance.

According to the fund commentary, these trends that are driving strong returns in industrials, materials, and other infrastructure-related companies are likely to continue into 2026, although global momentum is mixed.

  • Inflation is easing, labor markets are softening, and the U.S. Federal Reserve has pivoted to rate cuts. Despite tariff pressures, consumer sensitivity and constrained spending should limit inflation persistence. U.S. gross domestic product growth is projected to remain positive but moderate, with large-cap equities favored due to strong fundamental factors and AI-driven growth potential.
  • Global momentum is mixed. Europe and the United Kingdom face fiscal and structural headwinds, while Japan shows promise amid reforms. China struggles with property sector stress and cautious consumers, though offshore equities benefit from technology exposure. International bonds offer less attractive yields, and emerging markets debt faces uneven fundamental factors.

Risks to Consider Before Investing

One risk to consider with this fund is the call option strategy. According to the Q3 '25 Strategy Brief, about 34% of the portfolio assets had call options as of 9/30/25. If the option strategy is poorly executed it can detract from fund performance.

As discussed in the fund Q3 '25 Commentary, the option strategy underperformed during the quarter:

During the quarter the Fund’s option strategy had a negative impact on returns. The covered call strategy seeks to generate premiums and retain some potential for upside appreciation. The Fund implemented this strategy by typically writing call options on sector and regional ETFs, the selection and allocation of which resulted from an optimization intended to closely track the Fund’s reference index. The strike prices of the options written were typically at or near the money, with expiration dates around one month at inception.

Another risk is the global nature of the fund with more than 50% of fund holdings based outside the U.S. Voya’s investment process for the IDE fund consists of three steps, which are outlined in the Strategy Brief. If those steps are impacted by global events not predicted by their models there could be negative impacts to performance due to poor equity selections.

Voya's disciplined three step investment process begins with utilizing a quantitative model to create a target universe of stocks within the infrastructure related sectors within the index. Next, we use our global multi-factor stock selection models to help identify the most attractive securities by ranking each security relative to all other securities within its region-sector. We apply optimization techniques to construct a diversified portfolio of equity securities that help balance alpha and risk while staying within the targeted tracking error constraints.

Summary: Current Discount May Offer Good Entry Point

While IDE may already be a fund that is on your radar, it appears to me that it could be one to consider if you are an income investor who also likes to see your invested capital appreciate over time. The 10% distribution is currently well covered by NII and capital gains, the fund trades at a wider than average discount to NAV, and the global macro trends support continued growth in the Industrials, Infrastructure, and Materials market sectors. I rate IDE a Hold at this juncture, only because there is much uncertainty in the markets currently, which could jeopardize fund performance if the market volatility leads to further panic selling.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Power Solutions, USA Rare Earth, Archer Aviation And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser-covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between Nov. 7 and Nov. 13.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?


A 14% Dividend Yield Trading For Pennies On The Dollar: FS KKR Capital

Samuel Smith | Buy

I last discussed FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK) back in late July and took a closer look at its hefty dividend payout to see if it was sustainable. I dug deeper and revealed that I did not think the payout would be likely sustainable in an environment where the Fed pivoted to fairly aggressive rate cuts, given that it was struggling to cover it already and it had meaningful debt maturities coming up early next year, and also pointed out that the discount to NAV was not particularly attractive given that the company had subpar underwriting performance and had one of the least attractive fee structures among externally managed BDCs (BIZD). Therefore, I stated that I had "no desire to buy it at the time, barring a major dip in the stock price that would push the discount to a level that would be too steep to ignore."

Since then, the stock has fallen significantly, as it is now about 30% lower than it was when I wrote that article. As such, I am going to revisit FSK today to see if the discount to NAV is steep enough to determine if the stock is worth buying, especially given that management has signaled that a dividend cut is coming next year.

Read more here...


Power Solutions: In The Wrong Lane Of AI

Bashar Issa | Hold

Two years ago, Power Solutions International (PSIX) was an obscure micro-cap trading on the OTC. This year, the engine maker/fitter became a market darling, as investors wager that it turned into a winner in the furor of the AI infrastructure buildout.

On the surface, everything looks fine. Sales are growing, and cash is coming in. The debt maturities that threatened its going concern status discussed in my last article were extended. (Note: a going concern is a term used to refer to companies that are expected to continue existing.)

PSIX even released its first presentation in many years and took the opportunity to tout the AI narrative further, dropping "DATA CENTER" in bold and all caps at the end of a list of revenue growth drivers, after oil & gas recovery, product expansion, and grid resilience.

I think PSIX's revenue and earnings will grow. There is momentum in their earnings. Management's decision to expand production capacity is a capital commitment that signals demand growth.

At the same time, looking closer at the company's position and portfolio, I don't believe it is built for the megawatt scale of AI, notwithstanding its diesel portfolio, which is used for backup, the same portfolio that houses its flagship, 8.8-liter engine, the most suitable for AI applications, and the same one that experienced quality and warranty issues as recent as last year.

The limited scope of its Gas engine portfolio also gives it a disadvantage when compared to peers. PSIX is missing out on the current shift towards smaller gas engines, the alternative to large gas turbines, which are currently in short supply. This means that peers such as Cummins (CMI), Wartsila (OTCPK:WRTBF), Caterpillar (CAT), Generac, and others, with both diesel and gas engine portfolios, are better aligned with the AI boom.

Within this comparative lens, I think PSIX is overhyped, but holding the ticker in a well-diversified portfolio will give investors exposure to the current revenue and earnings growth momentum. The AI infrastructure boom is a wave lifting all boats, including PSIX.

Read more here...


USA Rare Earth: Back To Earth

Stone Fox Capital | Buy

USA Rare Earth, Inc. (USAR) sits at the junction of China controlling most rare earth mining and production capacity and the Trump Administration pushing for domestic sources. The stock has seen a very volatile move based on the ongoing trade war between the 2 major countries over crucial materials for the defense and semiconductor sectors. My investment thesis is Bullish on the stock after the dip back to the trend line and the current ramp of magnet production in the U.S., along with the acquisition of Less Common Metals, or LCM, in the U.K.

Read more here...


Prospect Capital: Strong Buy On 60% NAV Discount

The Asian Investor | Strong Buy

Prospect Capital Corporation (PSEC) reported core earnings (net investment income per share) exceeded analyst expectations by a large $0.06/share margin on Thursday and the investment firm seems to have stabilized its net investment income as well. Prospect Capital also announced new distributions of $0.045 per share for November 2025 through January 2026 and reported a low, although rising, non-accrual ratio for the September quarter. I continue to see Prospect Capital as a turnaround investment given its stabilizing NII, moderate dividend payout ratio and large discount to net asset value.

Read more here...


Archer's Pullback Is A Buy: The Dip Before Liftoff

Pythia Research | Strong Buy

The Q3 2025 earnings report from Archer Aviation Inc. (ACHR) marks the turning point in the eVTOL industry. My first bullish call played out nicely, with ACHR flying from $10.59 to $14.62 before crashing by 16% due to the pressure from the short seller report.

With its $126 million Hawthorne Airport purchase and fundraise of $650 million, Archer is transforming from an aircraft production company to an AI-driven aviation infrastructure solutions provider, turning its Hawthorne Airport into its flagship air taxi operation and AI lab in preparation for the Olympics scheduled in 2028 in Los Angeles.

I assign ACHR a strong buy rating as the company’s strategic execution, balance sheet strength, and accelerating regulatory progress position it to lead the commercialization of AI-powered urban air mobility and unlock substantial long-term shareholder value once FAA certification is achieved.

Read more here...


MSTY: Why This Extremely Risky ETF Is Worth My Capital

Dmytro Lebid | Hold

According to my previous articles, many may think that I belong to the camp of crypto enthusiasts. But I wouldn't call myself that. Although I have been familiar with the cryptocurrency market for a long time (since 2016), I look at many of its projects and digital assets with a great deal of skepticism. When I was first introduced to the features of the YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (MSTY), however, I couldn't believe that more than $2.6 billion in AUM could be invested in such a risky and aggressive investment strategy focused on generating high monthly income. Because this fund focuses on using an options strategy when working with Strategy Inc (MSTR) securities, the risk and volatility of this asset increase significantly. And that is probably the main reason why MSTY shows negative monthly dynamics.

Despite this, if you are willing to risk part of your capital in order to obtain a truly enormous yield (+230.43% per annum), then this asset is for you. A lot of analysts recommend selling it, and I agree with them because MSTY's momentum is still negative, maintaining a steady downward trend. Despite this, I want to find a few reasons to be optimistic about this asset. My recommendation to "Hold" is largely based on using a minimal amount of personal capital to purchase this asset with the aim of generating high passive income. Those risks associated with investing in MSTY do not allow this asset to be allocated a large portion of the investment portfolio (no more than 3%).

Read more here...


EOS Energy: Valued Like It Will Supply The Whole World

Andres Veurink | Sell

It can be a real divider writing a piece on EOS Energy (EOSE) as the opinions vary widely. Simply put, I am bearish on the stock right now for a number of reasons. The biggest reason being about how its strategy to reduce costs seems overly optimistic and that long-term forecasts point to the actual average selling prices declining a decent bit. Pair with an already high stock price, which doesn't imply any upside in either the near or medium term. This boils down to the company consisting of more risk than reward right now. Rating EOSE a sell.

VGT And XLK: Time To Cut Exposure To These Large ETFs

Alan Brochstein, CFA | Sell

The Vanguard Information Technology ETF (VGT) is a large ETF that has increased a lot in price. It is well-managed and is run by a company I highly admire at a cost to holders that is extremely low. Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) is also a large ETF that has gone up a lot. Both of these ETFs have a lot of followers at Seeking Alpha too. Both ETFs fell about 4% last week but are up a bit in 2025. In this article, I look into both ETFs and conclude that investors should reduce exposure.

VICI Properties Drives Shareholder Value With A New Transaction

Cash Flow Venue | Strong Buy

VICI Properties (VICI) has just delivered solid quarterly results, proving the business is as strong as ever, and announced a meaningful deal with Golden Entertainment (GDEN) that not only provides solid economics through attractive lease parameters but also allows VICI to diversify.

Before I go through this news, let me quickly comment on my previous ratings and most recent stock price performance. Since the first time I covered VICI, its stock price has remained quite flat, as it has increased by 3.5%. Still, VICI makes up for it with attractive dividends, driving the total return to 12.4%.

I had the pleasure of covering VICI a few times, and each time I either assigned it a "buy" or a "strong buy" rating. The last time I covered it I gave it a "strong buy" rating - while the timing wasn't perfect given VICI's stock price performance over the last month, I uphold my previously announced thesis.

In fact, the deal VICI has just shared, combined with solid quarterly results, and a more attractive entry level, makes me even more bullish. Therefore, I'll state right from the get-go - VICI remains a "strong buy" for me. Let me walk you through my reasoning step by step, starting with the VICI & GDEN deal.

Capital Southwest: Buy This 12% Yield While Market Is Asleep On Income

Gen Alpha | Buy

It’s a great time to be a high yield income investor, especially as the overall market remains expensive despite recent volatility. Unlike unrealized capital gains, dividends are yours to keep and can’t be clawed back by the market. As such, I’m more attuned to return of capital rather than return on capital. This way, my initial investment is de-risked after each and every dividend payment.

This brings me to Capital Southwest (CSWC), which has been a reliable dividend payer. I last covered CSWC back in September, highlighting the benefits of its internal management structure and sturdy portfolio. CSWC continues to trade at an attractive price at $20.77. As shown below, CSWC currently sits at about the middle of its 52-week range and carries a +12% dividend yield.

In this article, I revisit CSWC including recent business results, and discuss why this high yielding stock is currently a solid ‘Buy’ for income and total returns, so let’s get started!

JEPQ Isn't Outperforming Its Peers As There Are Better Options

Steven Fiorillo | Hold

The JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) was one of my favorite income focused ETFs for quite some time, but as the industry evolved, its strategy became less desirable. After doing a lot of research on its competitors, I am downgrading my investment thesis from very bullish to neutral on JEPQ as it's generating less yield and lower total returns than its competitors in 2025. I still believe that JEPQ can offer investors a combination of growth and income going forward but there are other strategies that are producing better returns and I have been deploying more capital toward them in 2025 rather than JEPQ. When it comes to building out an income producing portfolio I am looking to maximize my returns as I am expecting covered-call ETFs to underperform the market. JEPQ has been a strong investment for many investors since inception but the time has come for me to look elsewhere as the data no longer supports my previous bullishness. I am still holding my position and reinvesting all the income back into JEPQ but I will not be adding shares unless the underlying metrics improve.

Trinity Capital: Strange Dip Opens Up 14% Dividend Yield Opportunity

Pacifica Yield | Buy

Trinity Capital (TRIN) has seen its dividend yield expand in recent weeks, even as third-quarter earnings highlight healthy net investment income (NII) coverage. This has represented an opportunity for TRIN bulls to expand on positions in the BDC closer to its April "Liberation Day" tariff lows than its near-term peak. This dip was initially sparked by private credit concerns following the high-profile collapse of auto parts company First Brands Group (FBG) and auto lender Tricolor. TRIN is yet to experience a recovery, with its common shares down 11.4% from its near-term peak, swelling its dividend yield to 14%, an expansion from when I last covered the BDC. I view this dip as an opportunity; it was sparked by the extrapolation of the idiosyncratic risk events from two auto companies to the broader private credit space despite TRIN's primary focus on first lien senior secured credit to venture capital-backed technology firms.

TRIN last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.51 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $2.04 per share annualized for a 14% dividend yield. The BDC's dividend coverage is tighter, with fiscal 2025 third-quarter NII coming in at $0.52 per share for a 102% coverage ratio. Bears would be right to highlight that this was a sequential slip from 104% coverage in the second quarter. NII per share also dipped by 2 cents from $0.54 a year ago. data.title: ABN AMRO: Re-Rating Leaves Shares Fully Valued (Downgrade)
data.description:
While there's still time for this to change, 2025 looks set to be a great year for the European financial space. Dutch bank ABN AMRO (OTCPK:AAVMY) (OTC:ABMRF) has performed better than most, with its ADSs returning another ~10% since my last update in the summer, taking their year-to-date tally to around 125%.

For most of the time I have covered it, ABN has traded well below its book value. That has made its investment case relatively simple: assuming it could maintain a ~9%-10% return on equity, which is what management currently targets, then dividends and buybacks alone would be enough to power a double-digit annualized return for investors. With another set of quarterly numbers and a higher share price to digest, now is a good time to see whether this remains the case.

Credit Loss Provisioning Continues To Pad Earnings

ABN reported Q3 2025 results earlier in the week. Net profit after AT1 coupons came in at €559 million, beating consensus by around 4%. This mapped to a return on equity of 9.5%, right in the middle of management's target range. Technically, revenue of €2.169 billion missed by around 3%, but this was driven by volatile "other" income. At €2.141 billion, combined revenue from net interest income and fee income was in line with consensus. Net interest income ('NII') was a little weaker than expected, while fee income was slightly better.

Let's start with NII, which came in at €1.58 billion. This was down 6% year-on-year but up 1% quarter-on-quarter, both excluding the impact of M&A (this quarter included a full set of figures from the acquisition of private bank Hauck Aufhäuser Lampe—HAL hereafter).

ABN's net interest margin was flat sequentially, clocking in at 1.49%, while loans and deposits both rose by around 1% on an underlying basis—the latter driven by mortgage lending. Dutch house prices and transaction volumes continue to be supportive of this, as shown below.

Last time, I went over the slight headwind that the bank was seeing from its deposit hedging program (the so-called replicating portfolio). You can imagine this as being like a portfolio of fixed-income assets, funded by certain types of deposits (current accounts, for example). The headwind was coming from the reinvestment of these assets at lower yields, given declining Eurozone interest rates.

This headwind has now abated. Based on the forward curve and assuming static volumes, replicating portfolio income is expected to be flat through 2026. Consistent with this quarter's performance, I expect ABN's NII to more or less track volume growth for the next few quarters.

Fee income saw a better quarter, rising 17% year-on-year and 14% quarter-on-quarter to €561 million. While most of this was driven by the HAL deal, underlying growth was also strong, up 6.5% year-on-year and 3.5% quarter-on-quarter. I did say last time that buoyant stock markets were likely to feed into this period's results in a good way. This indeed was a factor, boosting asset levels (and asset-linked fees) in ABN's wealth business. Since markets remain buoyant and corporate fees tend to lull in the summer, I would expect another good quarter for fee income in Q4.

Excluding the contributions from HAL, the €2.141 billion that ABN generated in NII and fee revenue last quarter was around 1.5% higher than the year-ago period. Given this level of growth, delivering cost control is key to ABN's financial performance. Last quarter was decent on this front. Operating expenses clocked in at €1.409 billion, up 6% year-on-year and 7% quarter-on-quarter. HAL added around €65 million to ABN's costs last quarter, so underlying cost growth was much flatter. Indeed, management estimates that core costs have actually been falling, implying positive operating leverage.

Credit charges were also a bright spot, as ABN once booked another release last quarter. At €49 million, this was around €90 million better than analysts expected.

I do have mixed feelings about this. On the one hand, yes, clearly it is good that credit quality remains robust. The bank's Stage 3 (non-performing loans) and Stage 2 (performing loans, but showing signs of higher risk) ratios both fell 10bps sequentially last quarter, while the past due ratio also improved. Key macro indicators (Dutch unemployment, house prices, and so on) all remain supportive.

On the other hand, lower provisioning is flattering ABN's true profitability. Indeed, last quarter's performance mapped to a 7bps release when expressed relative to total loans. This is well below the 15-20bps charge that management aims to average across the cycle. For reference, if the midpoint of this range was applied to last quarter's income statement, ABN's return on equity would have been below 8%. Normalizing credit charges is a question of when rather than if, so this headwind is virtually guaranteed to occur at some point.

NIBC Deal Looks Smart

In addition to fresh financial results, ABN has also revealed more M&A news since my last piece, inking a deal to acquire NIBC, a commercial bank that is also based in the Netherlands.

The economics of this deal look fine. ABN will be paying 0.85x book value, equal to around €960 million based on NIBC's current balance sheet. NIBC is currently earning around €110 million in annual net income, and ABN is targeting around €100 million in post-tax cost synergies. While this is an aggressive assumption relative to NIBC's cost base (it booked around €100 million in operating expenses in H1 2025), management could undershoot this and still deliver a reasonable return on investment.

Valuation Now Less Compelling

The main problem that ABN now has is its valuation. The stock trades for €27.98 in Amsterdam as I type (around $32.50 per ADS), which is up from €25.10 at the time of my last piece ($29.14 per ADS) and €14.96 at the start of the year (around $15.30 per ADS). Meanwhile, book value has advanced by a more modest 0.5% since my last piece (3% since the start of the year) to €28.07 per share. As you can see, this move means the stock is now trading for around 1x book value. This is around 30% above its 10-year average.

Now, fundamentally, you would expect a company that earns its cost of equity to trade for around book value. So, assuming a 9%-10% cost of equity, which is in line with the long-term performance of the stock market, ABN could be around fair value, given it is also earning a 9%-10% ROE.

For the stock to be undervalued, this means ABN will have to improve its profitability. And like I said above, there is a headwind on the horizon from normalized credit charges. The company is running a Capital Markets Day later this month, where I fully expect management to lay out plans to boost the bank's ROE. I went over some of the possible routes it may go down last time.

The key point to take away is that when ABN was trading for less than book value, it wouldn't have mattered so much if management achieved this. The shares would have still offered investors a nice double-digit earnings yield, even on a ~9% ROE. After re-rating, this is no longer the case. And on that basis, I am shifting my rating to "Hold."

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Longest U.S. Government Shutdown Is Over: What Happened And Key Takeaways
data.description:
This U.S. Government shutdown was a doozy

At 43 days, we have finally concluded the longest shutdown in U.S. government history. The market didn't even flinch. There's really not a whole lot that can sway investors from the attractive flame of AI, the deal-making going on in the AI space, and the amount of layoffs occurring as a result of advancements in AI. Let's rehash the layoff announcements that did happen during this period, not including the quasi-federal layoffs that turned out to be more or less furloughs:

-Oct 28th: Amazon (AMZN) to cut 14,000 corporate jobs:

In June CEO Andy Jassy, who has aggressively sought to cut costs since becoming CEO in 2021, said that he anticipated generative AI would reduce Amazon’s corporate workforce in the next few years.

-Oct 28th: Meta Platforms (META) laying off more than 300:

Meta Platforms, the parent company of Facebook and Instagram, confirmed new layoffs affecting more than 300 Bay Area employees.

In notices filed with the California Employment Development Department, Meta said it will permanently lay off 318 employees in San Mateo County, effective Dec. 22.

-And this piece on the overall AI layoffs that occurred during the month of October:

US employers axed about 153,000 workers last month — making it the worst October for layoffs in two decades and bringing total firings for the year so far to over 1 million, according to a new report that noted many companies blamed the latest layoffs on AI.

Considering that not only was the government shut down, but also the fact that we just had the worst October layoff month in two decades, this market is taking bad news as good news. As long as margins grow, the market is assuming that the consumer will still have enough money one way or the other to continue buying these companies' products at the same clip or faster.

For now, the market participants have been rewarded, but at some point margin expansion from layoffs will hit a snag where the very employees they laid off will no longer be part of their consumer pool. Either through lack of liquidity, spite, or both.

What was this disagreement all about?

The root cause of the shutdown and the disagreement in the funding bill centered around the Affordable Care Act (ACA) AKA "Obamacare." This has to do with enhanced tax credits that were put in place during the Covid pandemic and were set to expire at the end of 2025. Even though they are tax credits, they get applied directly to the bill of enrollees in the month the expense is incurred rather than being a reduction to taxable income when someone files their taxes. These are a big deal for many.

Republicans also argued that the credits were extended to illegal aliens and, as an overall matter, were just too expensive to keep in their current form. Democrats argued that changing the premium tax credit to a reduced amount would result in unaffordable health care for many. This fight will be "to be continued" and we will certainly be talking about this once again at the end of the year.

Market performance during the shutdown

The government shutdown began at midnight on October 1st, 2025, and ended with President Trump signing the continuing resolution funding bill on November 12th, 2025. Let's observe how the broader markets performed. Here we will use the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY), Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), and SPDR® Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF Trust (DIA) as market total return proxies:

As we can see, everything is up. We also got another 25 basis point rate cut from Powell and the Federal Reserve on October 29th. This now brings the target on the short end range down to 3.75-4%. Did it move the ten-year (US10Y)?

The answer is a big no. The 10-year closed at 4.09% on the night of the shutdown and now sits at 4.142% despite the rate cut that occurred in the middle of the shutdown. Investors are still perfectly happy in cash equivalents that are now yielding about 3.25% for high-yield savings accounts to 4% for money markets and T-bill funds. Remember, the ten-year is the primary risk-free base-level tool that underwriters use to evaluate the riskiness of other types of debt, primarily corporate debt and mortgages.

The market may not care much about shutdowns when it comes to equities, but it increases the perception of credit risk and creates a higher interest rate demand. Even though the U.S. government has never defaulted, fixed-income investors still get skittish in a shutdown.

Will the 50-year mortgage now be a thing?

The 30-year fixed-rate mortgage (the most common type in the U.S.) did see an overall 10 basis point reduction from the start of the shutdown to current. While it's only 10 basis points, it has currently detached somewhat from the 10-year movement, at least in the near term. This could also be due to a demand problem or possibly just a factoring in of assumed future rate movements on the risk-free rate in the year to come.

At this point, Trump has finally seen Fed Chair Powell capitulate, but rates have had a meager reaction thus far. The newly announced idea of a 50-year mortgage is now out there, supported by both President Trump and Federal Housing Finance Agency Director Bill Pulte. The Internet is ripe for debate on whether this is a good or bad thing, observing that the compounded interest on a 50-year versus a 30-year mortgage results in a roughly doubling of interest paid over 50 years versus 30.

However, a home buyer is not restricted from paying these off early. U.S. mortgages almost never have pre-payment penalties built into them any longer. It still could be a better solution than renting for young individuals if the home they buy appreciates. This is positive for the home builders like Lennar (LEN), D.R. Horton (DHI), and Toll Brothers (TOL) if it all plays out.

Travel got hit hard

This was the big headline of the shutdown. Airport chaos, flight routes being cut down right around the Thanksgiving holiday, and questions over safety being abundant due to a lack of air traffic controllers and TSA agents, all of whom are employed by the Federal government.

Still amazingly so, major US travel brands like United Airlines (UAL), Delta Air Lines (DAL), Southwest Airlines (LUV), American Airlines (AAL), and Marriott International (MAR) did very well despite the negative headlines. Cancelled trips and travelers hitting the road versus flying were littering the airwaves. What pain these companies suffered will not be seen until this quarter is reported. For now they escaped with a "get out of jail free" card. I assume the numbers for this quarter are going to look messy.

We lost a big travel name during this shutdown

If there may be a canary in the coal for consumer budgets or maybe just the true effect of this shutdown, which led to travel disruptions, Airbnb (ABNB) competitor and Marriott partner Sonder Holdings (SOND) just went under:

In a Monday press release, the San Francisco company said it plans to file "a Chapter 7 liquidation of its US business," and "initiate insolvency proceedings" in other countries where it operates.

"We are devastated to reach a point where a liquidation is the only viable path forward," said Janice Sears, Sonder's interim CEO.

Sears cited "unexpected challenges" and delays in the company's partnership with Marriott, which was supposed to allow the hotel chain's loyal guests, known as Marriott Bonvoy members, to book Sonders rentals directly on the Bonvoy app or Marriott.com.

The news came one day after Marriott International ended its booking collaboration with Sonder, sending travelers who had made reservations with Sonder scrambling for new accommodations.

So, in essence, it looks like Marriott pulled the plug on Sonder. But why? Lack of travel demand? Did this 43-day travel snag play a role? This bankruptcy will surely be investigated further in the months to come.

Many employees are paycheck to paycheck

Here was a great survey of Federal workers during the shutdown and what they had to do to survive:

Terry Gerton: You and your company, Indeed Flex, have just done a recent survey that showed nearly 40% of furloughed federal workers are seeking flexible jobs within two weeks. What does that tell us about the shutdown and how it’s reshaping financial reality for folks?

Novo Constare: I think the reality of the shutdown is that it’s put a lot of people in severe financial hardship, where they’re struggling to pay key bills that matter to them, whether it be rent, energy, you name it. And we surveyed 1,000 adults, and as you mentioned, 40% of people were looking for extra work.

In summary

The paycheck-to-paycheck nature of many Federal workers, who are also voters, showed just how unpopular this government shutdown was. Several key Democrats (including the Mayor of New York) were elected this November that contained this overlap. President Trump also noted the shutdown contributed to Republican losses. While the government was shut down, the official Bureau of Labor Statistics on both private and public sector job losses was not published and may never be. When or if this true data gets leaked, expect a possible selloff because it cannot be good from the non-official stories we are hearing.

If I had to bet, the market still rallies into the end of the year, and a second shutdown is averted due to the unpopularity and possible blame being put on Republicans if November elections are any indicator. Both sides of the aisle may be more flexible by the end of the year on ACA premium credit extensions, which could also be good for the ultra-cheap Health Care sector.

data.title: 5 Top Utility Stocks Powering The Global Grid
data.description:
Electric Demand Boosts Utility Stocks

Utilities have been one of the highest-performing sectors in the U.S. and across the globe in 2025 as AI-fueled energy demand lifts corporate earnings and long-term outlooks. The market has been increasingly volatile as post-government shutdown optimism fades, rate cut odds decline, and fear of an AI bubble has investors fleeing for safety. High-yield utilities are in a rare sweet spot in today’s market — offering classic defensive downside protection and exposure to soaring electricity demand.

The S&P 500 utilities sector was one of the weaker performers last quarter, with Q225 earnings growth of only +1.2% year-over-year. But the picture has completely reversed this earnings season. With more than 90% of companies now reporting, utilities are among the top three sectors in Q325, delivering an earnings rebound of +23.2%. During the quarter, top utilities unveiled new agreements to provide data centers with power, requirements that continue to burgeon. Revising its forecast upward by 10%, Goldman Sachs projects a 175% increase in global data center power demand through 2030.

Source Link

While AI data centers are a powerful demand catalyst, they will still represent only about 2–3% of global electricity consumption by the end of the decade. The broader story is the steady rise in power requirements across sectors like transportation, industrial, commercial, and residential segments. Overall global electricity consumption is forecast to reach roughly 36,602 TWh by 2030, according to the IAEA — an increase of about 20% from 2024 levels.

Not only do utility stocks stand to gain from these structural demand trends, but some may be safe havens in rough times, along with providing stable income. Utilities have outperformed during slowdowns and recessions, with demand for electricity, water, and gas remaining stable – regardless of economic conditions. I identified five utility stocks that could potentially meet these objectives for investors while helping to power the global grid.

How I Selected the 5 Top Utility Stocks Powering the Grid

I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener to identify the top quant-rated stocks in the sector and targeted securities in the electric utilities and multi-utilities industries. The utilities selected include companies that manage electricity networks and produce energy from renewable and traditional sources. The companies are headquartered in the U.S., Brazil, Spain, Italy, and Bermuda, and have operations spanning five continents and dozens of countries. My top utility picks have excellent all-around investment fundamentals, positive long-term earnings growth potential, and solid dividend yields.

1. Axia Energia (AXIA)

  • Market Capitalization: $26.32B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Utilities

  • Industry: Electric Utilities

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 1 out of 111

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 1 out of 42

In a bid to establish an independent and future-oriented brand, Brazilian state power giant Eletrobras changed its name to Axia Energia and began trading under the ticker AXIA on Nov. 10. The new name, which means “value” in Greek, expresses the company's purpose: To be an “axis” that ensures predictability and sustainable performance. The company underwent a privatization process in the past three years, reducing the government ownership stake to 45%, and it has revamped its portfolio to 100% clean energy sources, including hydropower and wind, and is targeting net zero emissions by 2030.

Axia operates 82 plants, totaling 44.4 GW of installed capacity, which accounts for 17% of the national total. Axia has achieved exceptional price performance over the past year, given its strategic and financial streamlining and focus on energy storage technology, earning an A+ Momentum Grade and ranking No. 1 among quant-rated stocks in both the utilities sector and the electric utilities industry.

Axia’s Q3 net revenue fell 9.4% – largely due to the divestment of thermal and nuclear assets – offsetting higher transmission segment sales. Axia's energy trading business served 813 clients in the third quarter, up 16% YoY, with the free market segment up 18% to 723. Looking ahead, the average price of Axia’s contracted power sales is expected to rise steadily in 2026 and 2027, a trend in line with positive sentiment from Wall Street analysts. According to consensus estimates, Axia’s revenue is projected to grow by ~6.5% to $7.39B in 2025 and over 10% in each of the following two fiscal years.

Axia Consensus Revenue Estimates FY25-FY27

Axia’s A+ Growth Grade is also driven by exceptional growth in revenue and operating cash flow in the trailing twelve months. Operating cash flow is up 88% in the past year, an impressive rate compared to the sector’s 4%. Axia’s B Profitability Grade is primarily supported by a sturdy cash from operations of $3.15B, 126% above the sector median. Axia is trading at an attractive valuation with an EV/EBITDA of 9x – a discount to the sector of approximately 24%. Axia’s valuation is also supported by an 8.46% dividend yield. However, Axia’s stock has a warning indicating it might be at high risk of cutting its dividend argely due to high payout ratios and weak interest coverage. While its dividend payout timing is less consistent than the sector, the company has steadily improved over the past year.

Axia is riding a surging electricity wave and boosting clients in the free market segment while generating strong cash flow and offering a high dividend yield. The top quant-rated stock in the sector, Axia is a great option to consider for investors looking to boost the utility segment of their portfolios.

2. Iberdrola, S.A. (OTCPK:IBDRY)

  • Market Capitalization: $135.81B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Utilities

  • Industry: Electric Utilities

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 3 out of 111

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 3 out of 42

Iberdrola, S.A., is a global producer and distributor of electricity, generating most of its power from renewable sources and operating regulated grids in Spain, the U.S., the UK, Mexico, and Brazil. The company has steadily repositioned its asset base toward wind, hydro, and solar, giving it long-term upside as global electrification accelerates. Over 78% of its 57.45 GW of installed capacity consists of renewable energy sources, with almost half coming from onshore and offshore wind generation.

Data centers are emerging as a major demand driver for Iberdrola. The company already supplies more than 12 TWh of renewable power annually to major tech clients through U.S. PPAs. The stock has crushed the market and sector in the past year and has continued to sustain momentum, supported by positive earnings results last month.

In the first nine months of 2025, Iberdrola adjusted net profit grew by 16.6% YoY and revenue by +2.2%, largely driven by robust operating performance in the company’s Networks business, where EBITDA rose by 26%. This was largely the result of a higher rate base driven by investment and improved regulatory frameworks, along with positive rate adjustments in the United States and Brazil.

Iberdrola’s operating profit, free cash flow, and total return on capital are key metrics driving its A+ Profitability Grade - along with impressive cash from operations of $15.02B in the past year. EPS long-term forward growth rate of 8% is underpinning a ‘B+’ Growth Grade. Wall Street analysts are expecting the company to finish the year strong, with FY25 EPS projected to rise by 22% and revenue by 17%.

Iberdrola has an average valuation grade, trading at 19x earnings, although its PEG of 2.36 and EV/EBITDA of 10.9x represent discounts to the sector of around 11% and 8%. IBDRY offers a 3.45% dividend yield, 31% five-year growth rate, and 18 consecutive years of payments. Strategically poised to ride electrification megatrends, Iberdrola’s excellent profitability and growth projections underpin a Strong Buy quant rating.

3. Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. Limited Partnership Units (BIP)

  • Market Capitalization: $16.62B

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Sector: Utilities

  • Industry: Multi-Utilities

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 6 out of 111

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/14/2025): 1 out of 20

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners owns a global portfolio of long-life utilities, transport, midstream and data assets spread across North America, South America, Europe, Asia, and Australia, and is positioning itself for rising AI infrastructure demand. The stock price has lagged the sector in the past year but has seen a surge in momentum in the last three months, boosted by strong Q3 earnings results.

In Q325, Brookfield Infrastructure recorded revenue of $5.98B (+13.38% YoY) and FFO of $654M (+10% YoY), driven by strong operational performance, organic growth, and realized gains from capital recycling activities. The company secured six new investments, including a $5B project with Bloom Energy Corporation (BE) to install up to 1 GW of power solutions for data centers and AI factories. Brookfield Infrastructure sees a huge opportunity in the AI infrastructure boom, CEO Samuel Pollock said in the earnings call:

We're also seeing a significant new growth vertical emerging from the rapid build-out of AI infrastructure, a $7 trillion opportunity set that remains in its early stages and continues to expand. We expect to deploy up to $500 million annually into AI-related infrastructure in the coming years with AI factories and behind-the-meter power solutions representing a natural compelling extension of our investment activities. These growth factors paired with a macroeconomic backdrop, that is trending very favorably, set the stage for BIP's FFO per unit growth to inflect higher.”

BIP’s A- Growth Grade is underpinned by excellent growth in operating cash flow, capex, ROE, and forward EPS growth of 13%. BIP showcases robust EBITDA and operating margins, along with capex/sales of 49%, indicating it is reinvesting at a solid rate. BIP offers a dividend yielding 4.83% with a 5Y growth rate of 5.83% and 16 years of consecutive payments. Wall Street analysts project that the dividend will continue growing steadily over the next two years.

BIP is trading at high earnings multiples, but at a significant discount relative to cash flow and revenue. Standing to benefit from soaring global AI infrastructure requirements, BIP offers profitable growth at a value, making it a top utilities stock.
